questions.question: Explain the difference between Pay-as-you-go and Reserved Instance pricing models in cloud computing.
keyPoints: Pay-as-you-go is hourly/per-second billing with no commitment
Reserved Instances require 1-3 year commitment for 60-70% discount
PAYG offers flexibility but higher unit cost
Reserved is best for predictable, steady-state workloads

questions.question: What is Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)? List at least four components considered in TCO calculation.
keyPoints: TCO is complete cost of owning IT infrastructure over its lifetime
Components: Hardware/Software acquisition
Facilities (datacenter space, power, cooling)
Personnel (IT staff salaries)
Maintenance and support costs
Operational expenses including utilities

questions.question: Describe horizontal scaling and vertical scaling with an example of each.
keyPoints: Vertical scaling (scale-up): Adding more CPU/RAM to existing server
Example: Upgrading from 4-core to 8-core instance
Horizontal scaling (scale-out): Adding more machines/instances
Example: Adding more web servers behind load balancer
Vertical has hardware limits, horizontal is more elastic

questions.question: List and briefly explain four cloud cost optimization strategies.
keyPoints: Right-sizing: Matching instance size to actual workload needs
Reserved Instances: Using commitment for predictable workloads
Auto-scaling: Adding/removing capacity based on demand
Spot Instances: Using spare capacity for fault-tolerant workloads
Removing idle resources: Terminating unused instances

questions.question: What is the difference between CapEx and OpEx? How does cloud computing shift the model?
keyPoints: CapEx: Large upfront capital investment in physical assets
OpEx: Pay-as-you-go operational expenses
Traditional IT uses CapEx model with hardware purchases
Cloud uses OpEx model with pay-per-use pricing
OpEx offers flexibility, lower upfront costs, and easier scaling

questions.question: Explain the concept of economies of scale in cloud computing.
keyPoints: Economies of scale means lower per-unit costs with increased production
Cloud providers spread fixed infrastructure costs over millions of users
Massive datacenters achieve higher utilization rates
Providers pass savings to customers through competitive pricing
Results in cost advantages impossible for individual companies

questions.question: What is auto-scaling? How does it help in cost optimization?
keyPoints: Auto-scaling automatically adjusts compute instances based on metrics
Metrics include CPU utilization, memory usage, request count
Adds instances during high demand to maintain performance
Removes instances during low demand to avoid unnecessary costs
Results in paying only for needed capacity

questions.question: A company is planning to migrate their on-premise infrastructure to cloud. Discuss the factors they should consider for calculating ROI of cloud migration.
keyPoints: Define baseline TCO of on-premise: hardware, software, facilities, personnel, maintenance
Calculate cloud migration costs: migration tools, training, temporary downtime
Identify benefits: infrastructure cost savings, reduced maintenance, improved agility
Consider ongoing cloud costs: compute, storage, data transfer, support
Calculate: ROI = (Net Benefits / Total Migration Cost) x 100
Consider intangibles: scalability, reliability, global reach, faster deployment
Time horizon: typically 3-5 years for meaningful comparison
Hidden costs: data transfer out, API calls, cross-region traffic

questions.question: Explain the various cloud pricing models. For each model, identify the type of workload it is best suited for and justify your answer.
keyPoints: On-demand/Pay-as-you-go: Best for unpredictable, short-term workloads; no commitment, pay per hour/second
Reserved Instances: Best for predictable, steady-state workloads; 1-3 year commitment gives 60-70% discount
Spot/Premium Instances: Best for fault-tolerant, batch processing workloads; up to 90% discount but can be interrupted
Serverless: Best for event-driven, variable workloads; pay per request and execution time
Savings Plans: Flexible commitment across instance families; good for predictable spend with variable usage
Dedicated Hosts: Best for compliance/regulatory needs; dedicated physical server

questions.question: Design a cost-optimized cloud architecture for an e-commerce website that experiences significant traffic variation between weekdays and weekends.
keyPoints: Use Reserved Instances for baseline capacity (database, web server minimum)
Implement auto-scaling for web tier based on CPU/requests during peak hours
Use Spot Instances for batch processing (order processing, analytics)
Implement CDN for static content delivery to reduce bandwidth costs
Use serverless for functions (payment processing, notifications)
Set up budget alerts and cost monitoring
Right-size databases based on actual usage patterns
Use lifecycle policies for storage (move old data to cheaper tiers)
Consider multi-region deployment closer to users
